It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Key finding: Level 1's white bar ended at 28px, and level 2's white bar started at 64px. So the white bar resets to 64 at the start of each level.
In level 1, the white bar went from 64 to 28 (decrease of 36) in 19 actions, completing the level. The level was completed by clicking at (19, 44), which was near the blue octagon's position.
In level 2, the white bar went from 64 to 6 (decrease of 58) in 36 actions. The level hasn't been completed yet.
Wait, but the level 1 completion action was MOUSE(row=19, col=44), which clicked on the blue octagon. Maybe the goal is to click on the blue octagon to complete the level?
But in level 1, the white bar was at 28 when the level was completed. In level 2, the white bar is at 6. Maybe the level completes when the white bar reaches a certain threshold, or when a specific action is taken.
Let me check what happened when I clicked on the yellow square. The white bar stayed at 6, which is unusual. Maybe clicking on the yellow square is a special action that triggers something.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the level 1 completion. The action was MOUSE(row=19, col=44), and the level changed from 1 to 2. Let me check what was at (19, 44) in level 1.
In level 1, the blue octagon was at approximately (11-19, 44-52). So (19, 44) is at the bottom-left corner of the blue octagon. Clicking there completed the level.
In level 2, the blue octagon is at approximately (23-31, 29-37). Maybe I need to click on the blue octagon to complete the level, but only after the white bar reaches a certain threshold.
Actually, let me reconsider. The white bar in level 1 went from 64 to 28, and the level was completed. In level 2, the white bar went from 64 to 6. If the target is the same (28), then the level should have been completed when the white bar reached 28. But it wasn't.
So the target might be different for each level. In level 1, the target was 28. In level 2, the target might be 0 or some other value.
Actually, I just realized something. The white bar didn't decrease on the last action (clicking on the yellow square). This is the first time it didn't decrease. Maybe clicking on the yellow square is a special action that triggers something, or maybe it's an invalid action that doesn't count.
Let me check the last_action_result to see if the board changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check last action result
print(f"Last action result: {last_action_result}")
print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed', 'N/A')}")

# Also check if clicking on the blue shape does something
# Blue shape is at (23-31, 29-37)
# Let me try clicking at the center of the blue shape

print("\nTrying to click on blue shape center...")
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 27, 'col': 33}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
